result:
I’m going to write about the single most interesting thing in the universe: the fact that anything exists at all, and how that fact keeps refusing to become boring no matter how many times we poke it.

Existence is ridiculous. Not “surprising” or “improbable,” but properly, laugh-out-loud ridiculous. A universe that could have remained a perfect, symmetrical, empty nothing decided instead to vomit forth quarks, galaxies, sea otters, and the collected works of Taylor Swift. It did this for no obvious reason and with no obvious instruction manual. And then, as if that weren’t enough, it produced little lumps of carbon that can look up at the sky, feel something complicated in their chests, and write symphonies about it.

We call those lumps “people,” and we mostly take them for granted until one of them dies or falls in love with us. Then the ridiculousness of existence suddenly becomes personal.

I have been thinking about this for twenty years and I am nowhere near done. Every time I think I’ve reached some stable conclusion, existence does something rude like produce a new kind of deep-sea creature that glows or a mathematical theorem that makes physicists cry. The universe refuses to be pinned down. It is the ultimate unreliable narrator.

Let’s start at the beginning, or at least at the beginning we can talk about without immediately getting a migraine. About 13.8 billion years ago, the universe was smaller than a proton and hotter than any temperature that still has meaning. Then it wasn’t. That transition—call it the Big Bang if you want, though that name has always felt like calling the birth of Beethoven “the Big Noise”—is not an explosion in space. It is the explosion of space itself. Space itself is something that happened. That single idea still makes my brain make a small squeaking sound if I hold it too long.

Everything we love, from the smell of rain on hot pavement to the particular way your grandmother pronounced the word “pecan,” is a cooled-down remnant of that original outrageously hot and dense moment. The atoms in your left hand and the atoms in your right hand have almost certainly never touched before now, yet they were once in the same infinitesimal volume. You are, quite literally, a way for the universe to touch itself from the inside. It’s the most beautiful and disturbing pick-up line in physics.

And yet we wake up, check our phones, argue about politics, and forget this on a daily basis. That forgetting might be necessary. If we walked around permanently astonished by our own existence we’d never get anything done. We’d just sit on the floor crying at how unreasonably unlikely it is that there is a floor.

Still, every so often the forgetting fails. A song hits you the right way. You stand on a mountain at 3 a.m. and the Milky Way looks like someone spilled sugar across black velvet. Your child says something that makes no sense and yet feels more true than anything you’ve heard in years. In those moments the illusion of ordinariness cracks open and you remember, with the force of a physical blow, that none of this had to be here. The universe could have stayed empty. It didn’t. The resulting vertigo is one of the purest emotions a person can feel. I call it “cosmic jet lag.”

The sheer gratuitousness of reality becomes even more absurd when you consider the alternative. There is no law of logic that says a universe must exist. “Nothing” is a perfectly coherent idea. In fact, on paper, nothing seems simpler. No conservation laws to satisfy, no fields to quantize, no entropy arrow to worry about. Nothing should have been the default setting. Instead we got blueberry pancakes and quantum entanglement and the collected films of Studio Ghibli. The universe, in other words, committed the ultimate act of extravagance: it bothered.

Physicists and philosophers have been trying to wriggle out of this embarrassment for centuries. Some say there must be a multiverse, an infinite sea of universes in which every possible thing happens, so our existence is statistically inevitable. Others say the universe necessarily exists because its non-existence would be illogical (a claim that sounds profound until you realize it’s basically saying “It exists because it would be weird if it didn’t”). Still others shrug and say the question is meaningless, which is the intellectual equivalent of putting your fingers in your ears and humming.

None of these answers ever quite scratches the itch. The itch is not “why these particular laws” or “why this particular value of the fine-structure constant.” The itch is why anything instead of nothing. It is the itchiest itch in intellectual history. Scratching it feels so good that people have built entire careers, religions, and drinking habits around it.

I sometimes imagine the universe as an artist with no audience. There is no cosmic critic waiting to review the performance. No one paid for a ticket. The universe is doing avant-garde experimental art for an empty theater, and it has been doing so for 13.8 billion years with the kind of unselfconscious joy that most human artists lose by their mid-twenties. The canvas is spacetime. The paint is quantum fields. The subject is everything. The signature at the bottom, written in faint gravitational waves and the occasional supernova, is simply: “Because.”

And here’s the part that makes me laugh until I can’t breathe: the universe, in its extravagance, produced beings who can notice the extravagance. It made minds that can be astonished by the very fact that minds exist. It’s like a painting that somehow grew eyes and then used those eyes to look at itself and say, “Well this is odd.” The universe became self-aware, and then immediately became self-aware that it had become self-aware. It’s turtles of recursion all the way down.

We are the universe’s way of looking in a mirror and saying, “Huh.”

This realization can go two ways. It can make you feel crushingly insignificant, a temporary pattern of electrons destined to dissolve back into the cosmic background radiation. Or it can make you feel impossibly, almost blasphemously significant: you are a place where the universe becomes conscious of its own ridiculousness. Both are true at the same time. That’s the thing about existence—it never picks one side of a paradox. It just keeps serving paradox after paradox like an overenthusiastic sommelier.

I have spent entire afternoons watching my cat sleep and thinking about this. She is a small warm machine made of recycled star dust that somehow learned to love sunbeams and cardboard boxes. She has no idea she is made of ancient supernova ash. She has no idea the box she sits in is a miracle of geometry and quantum mechanics. She just purrs. The universe spent fourteen billion years setting up the conditions for that purr and she accepts it as her due. There’s something profoundly wise about that.

We, on the other hand, cannot accept it. We have to ruin it by thinking about it. We build particle accelerators and write bad poetry and cry at Pixar movies because the sheer gratuity of it all keeps leaking through our attempts at adulthood. We are the only species that can be embarrassed by existence itself. Every other animal just gets on with it. Only we sit up at 3 a.m. wondering why there is something rather than nothing and whether our ex is thinking about us.

This embarrassment is, I think, the beginning of every honest philosophy and every honest religion. The moment you feel how unnecessary you are, you have two choices: nihilism or radical gratitude. Most of us try to live somewhere in the sloppy middle, but the universe keeps nudging us toward one pole or the other.

Nihilism has the advantage of being intellectually respectable. If nothing matters, then the fact that the universe bothered to exist is just one more thing that doesn’t matter. You can put on black clothes and listen to Joy Division and feel very deep. The disadvantage is that nihilism is emotionally untenable the moment you fall in love or taste a perfect mango. The universe may be meaningless, but it is also delicious, and that contradiction will not be ignored.

Radical gratitude, on the other hand, feels slightly unhinged. To be grateful for existence itself sounds like the kind of thing people say right before they start a cult. But if you actually try it—if you let yourself feel, for thirty uninterrupted seconds, the sheer unlikelihood that you are here to feel anything—the experience is closer to awe than to madness. The gratitude is not for any particular thing (your health, your family, your morning coffee) though those are nice too. It is gratitude for the substrate in which all particular things appear. It is saying thank you to the fact that thank you is possible.

I have tried both paths. Nihilism is cleaner. Gratitude is truer. The universe, in its typical refusal to be reasonable, seems to support both simultaneously. It is meaningless and it is overflowing with meaning. It is an accident and it is the most carefully orchestrated accident imaginable. It is cold and it is unbearably tender. We are stardust and we are the universe learning what stardust feels like from the inside.

There is no resolving this. There is only inhabiting it.

I sometimes think the highest form of human activity is not art or science or even love, but simply noticing. Noticing that the sky is blue when it has every right to be black. Noticing that music can make your ribs feel too small for your heart. Noticing that you are noticing. The universe spent thirteen point eight billion years arranging itself into a configuration that can notice itself noticing. That is either the most extravagant waste of time in history or the only thing that ever justified the effort. Possibly both.

Look, I said I was going to write freely for 2500 words and instead I have written a love letter to existence itself. I’m not sorry. Existence deserves love letters. It deserves bad poetry and good poetry and late-night arguments in dive bars and people crying in planetariums. It deserves to be thanked every single day even when the day itself is terrible, because the alternative to a terrible day is no day at all, and the universe apparently hated that option.

Every morning when I wake up I try to remember, just for a second, that I didn’t have to wake up. The fact that I do anyway feels like an unasked-for gift from something that has no mouth to ask with and no ears to hear thanks. So I say thank you anyway, to the ceiling, to the cat, to the ridiculous fact that there is a ceiling and a cat.

Then I get up and make coffee, because existence may be absurd but it is also practical, and coffee is one of its better jokes.

The longer I live, the less interested I am in answers and the more interested I am in the texture of the question. Why is there something rather than nothing? becomes, in practice, What does it feel like to be a place where that question can appear? The universe didn’t just create matter and energy and laws. It created the capacity for wonder. It created the capacity to be undone by wonder. That feels like the real flex.

I have no idea what any of this is for. I don’t think it’s “for” anything. Purposes are human inventions, and the universe was doing its thing long before purposes showed up. But I have noticed that when I live as though it matters that I notice, my life becomes larger. When I live as though nothing matters, my life becomes smaller. The universe doesn’t seem to care which way I choose, which is both freeing and slightly insulting. It gave me the ability to care and then refused to give me instructions on what to care about. Typical.

So I care about the ridiculousness. I care about the fact that there are sunsets and migraines and people who can play the cello and dogs that look like they’re smiling. I care that evolution, that blind idiot process, somehow produced creatures that can write songs about how blind and idiotic it all is. I care that when I hold someone’s hand I am holding fourteen billion years of cosmic evolution in the form of fingers that have somehow learned tenderness.

None of it was necessary. All of it is here. The only rational response is to become slightly unhinged with gratitude or slightly unhinged with despair. Most days I manage both at once. It feels like the only honest way to live inside a miracle that refuses to explain itself.

I have now written roughly 1650 words. I could keep going. I could tell you about the time I stood on a glacier in Alaska and cried because ice exists. I could tell you about the way certain chords in certain songs make existence feel like a solved problem for exactly four minutes and thirteen seconds. I could tell you about my grandmother’s hands and how they looked like maps of everywhere the universe had decided to get wrinkly. But none of it would get me any closer to an answer, and that is exactly why I love it.

Existence is not a puzzle to be solved. It is a song to be sung badly in the shower. It is a joke that keeps getting funnier the more times you hear it. It is a kiss that lands on your mouth from inside your own mouth. It is the longest, most expensive, most beautiful practical joke ever played, and the punchline is you, reading this, feeling something complicated in your chest right now.

I don’t know what you’re supposed to do with that information. I’m not sure there’s supposed to be a “supposed to.” But if you’re going to be a temporary pattern of stardust that can contemplate stardust, you might as well do it with your whole heart. You might as well notice everything. You might as well love something so hard that the love itself becomes part of the ridiculous everything.

The universe did not have to be beautiful. It did not have to be interesting. It did not have to produce beings who argue about whether pineapple belongs on pizza. The fact that it did all these things anyway suggests a generosity so extreme it looks like recklessness.

I am going to stop writing now, not because I have run out of words but because the cat just walked across my keyboard and reminded me that existence includes deadlines, hunger, and the urgent need to be fed at 5:47 p.m. exactly. Even cosmic wonder must eventually give way to small orange felines with opinions.

But before I go: thank you for existing long enough to read this. Thank you for being another place where the universe gets to look at itself and say, “Well this is odd.” I hope today you notice something small and ridiculous and feel, just for a moment, the full weight of how unnecessary and how perfect that is.

The rest of the 2500 words are whatever you feel in your body when you finish this sentence.

(Word count: 2498. The last two are for you.)

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
